 {"id":"bd-1d3","title":"Implement TUI snapshot testing infrastructure","description":"# Implement TUI snapshot testing infrastructure

## Goal
Create infrastructure for testing the interactive TUI by capturing terminal output
and comparing against golden snapshots, with detailed logging.

## Background
src/interactive.rs is 2842 lines with only 2 trivial tests (<0.1% coverage).
This is the most user-visible code and needs comprehensive testing.

## Approach: Snapshot Testing

### Why Snapshots?
- TUI output is complex (ANSI codes, layout)
- Pixel-perfect testing impractical
- Snapshots capture "expected output" simply
- Easy to update when intentional changes made

### Snapshot Format
```
// tests/snapshots/tui_initial_state.snap
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Pi - AI Coding Agent                │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                     │
│ > [cursor]                          │
│                                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Model: claude-sonnet-4   Tokens: 0  │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Logging Requirements
- Use TestLogger (bd-3ml) for each snapshot test.
- Log view size, theme name, state flags, and snapshot name.
- On mismatch, dump the rendered view (ANSI-stripped) + diff context.

## Determinism Requirements
- Force terminal size (80x24) and disable time-based animations/spinners in test mode.
- Strip ANSI codes for snapshots.
- Use a fixed default theme and locale.

## Implementation

### Snapshot Library
Use insta crate for Rust snapshot testing:
```toml
[dev-dependencies]
insta = { version = "1", features = ["filters"] }
```

### Test Structure
```rust
// tests/tui_snapshot.rs

use insta::assert_snapshot;
use pi::interactive::PiApp;

#[test]
fn test_initial_state() {
    let app = PiApp::new(Config::default());
    let view = app.view();
    assert_snapshot!("initial_state", strip_ansi(&view));
}
```

### ANSI Code Handling
Strip ANSI codes for deterministic snapshots:
```rust
fn strip_ansi(s: &str) -> String {
    let re = regex::Regex::new(r"\x1b\[[0-9;]*[a-zA-Z]").unwrap();
    re.replace_all(s, "").to_string()
}
```

### Width/Height Normalization
Force consistent terminal size:
```rust
fn normalized_view(app: &PiApp) -> String {
    app.view_with_size(80, 24)
}
```

## Test Scenarios

### Layout Tests
1. Initial empty state
2. Single user message
3. Single assistant message
4. Conversation with multiple messages
5. Long message with wrapping
6. Scrolled viewport

### State Tests
7. Idle state
8. Streaming text
9. Streaming thinking
10. Tool execution in progress
11. Error display
12. Slash command help

### Input Tests
13. Text in input field
14. Multi-line input
15. History navigation
16. Cursor positioning

## Dependencies
None (can start independently)

## Files
- tests/tui_snapshot.rs
- tests/snapshots/*.snap (generated)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] insta crate integrated
- [ ] ANSI stripping works
- [ ] Terminal size normalized
- [ ] 15+ snapshot tests
- [ ] Logs include snapshot metadata
- [ ] cargo insta test works
- [ ] CI validates snapshots","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:35:28.554490342Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T08:42:46.796475700Z","closed_at":"2026-02-03T08:42:46.796412182Z","close_reason":"Added tui_snapshot tests + insta snapshots; suite green","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1d3","depends_on_id":"bd-26s","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:35:28.561095760Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1d3","depends_on_id":"bd-3ml","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T05:44:35.286107381Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":17,"issue_id":"bd-1d3","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Progress: added TUI snapshot test harness (tests/tui_snapshot.rs) using TestHarness + insta; added PiApp::set_terminal_size and PI_TEST_MODE init gating for animations; added dev-dep insta.  {"id":"bd-1mh","title":"Update session picker to use SQLite index","description":"# Update session picker to use SQLite index

## Goal
Replace filesystem walking in session picker with fast SQLite index queries.

## Current Implementation (src/session_picker.rs)
Currently scans filesystem:
```rust
// Slow: walks all session directories
let sessions = walkdir::WalkDir::new(&sessions_dir)
    .into_iter()
    .filter_map(|e| e.ok())
    .filter(|e| e.path().extension() == Some("jsonl"))
    .collect();
```

## New Implementation
```rust
use crate::session_index::SessionIndex;

impl SessionPicker {
    pub fn load_sessions(cwd: Option<&str>) -> Result<Vec<SessionMeta>> {
        let index = SessionIndex::open_default()?;
        index.list_sessions(cwd)
    }
}
```

## UI Changes
- Display indexed metadata directly (no file reads needed)
- Show message count from index
- Show session name from index
- Faster render (no filesystem I/O)

## Fallback Behavior
If index unavailable or empty:
```rust
fn load_sessions_with_fallback(cwd: Option<&str>) -> Result<Vec<SessionMeta>> {
    // Try index first
    if let Ok(index) = SessionIndex::open_default() {
        if let Ok(sessions) = index.list_sessions(cwd) {
            if !sessions.is_empty() {
                return Ok(sessions);
            }
        }
    }
    
    // Fallback to filesystem (and populate index)
    let sessions = load_sessions_from_filesystem(cwd)?;
    // Optionally trigger reindex in background
    Ok(sessions)
}
```

## Dependencies
- bd-3nz (indexing must work first)

## Testing
- Test: Session picker shows indexed sessions
- Test: Picker falls back to filesystem if needed
- Test: Performance improved (measure time)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Picker queries index by default
- [ ] Fallback to filesystem works
- [ ] UI displays index metadata
- [ ] Noticeably faster for 50+ sessions","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:38:46.861448590Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T03:38:59.101819926Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1mh","depends_on_id":"bd-346","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:38:46.870643664Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1mh","depends_on_id":"bd-3nz","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:38:59.101795250Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-9sa","title":"Implement asupersync HTTP client wrapper","description":"# Implement asupersync HTTP client wrapper

## Goal
Create a functional HTTP client in src/http/client.rs using asupersync APIs.

## Current State
src/http/client.rs has:
- Client struct (empty)
- RequestBuilder struct with method/url/headers/body
- RequestBuilder::send() returns Err("asupersync HTTP client not yet implemented")
- RequestBuilder::json() silently ignores serialization errors

## Implementation Requirements

### Client API
```rust
use asupersync::{Cx, http::client::HttpClient};
use asupersync::tls::TlsConnectorBuilder;

pub struct Client {
    inner: HttpClient,
}

impl Client {
    pub fn new() -> Result<Self, Error> {
        // Create TLS connector with native roots
        let tls = TlsConnectorBuilder::new()
            .with_native_roots()?
            .build()?;
        let inner = HttpClient::new(tls);
        Ok(Self { inner })
    }
    
    pub fn request(&self, method: Method, url: &str) -> RequestBuilder {
        RequestBuilder::new(self, method, url)
    }
    
    pub fn get(&self, url: &str) -> RequestBuilder { ... }
    pub fn post(&self, url: &str) -> RequestBuilder { ... }
}
```

### RequestBuilder API
```rust
impl RequestBuilder {
    pub fn header(mut self, key: &str, value: &str) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn json<T: Serialize>(mut self, body: &T) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn body(mut self, bytes: Vec<u8>) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn timeout(mut self, duration: Duration) -> Self { ... }
    
    pub async fn send(self, cx: &Cx) -> Result<Response, Error> {
        // Actually send the request using asupersync
    }
}
```

### Response API
```rust
pub struct Response {
    status: StatusCode,
    headers: Headers,
    body: BodyReader,  // Async streaming body
}

impl Response {
    pub fn status(&self) -> StatusCode { ... }
    pub fn headers(&self) -> &Headers { ... }
    pub async fn text(self, cx: &Cx) -> Result<String, Error> { ... }
    pub async fn json<T: DeserializeOwned>(self, cx: &Cx) -> Result<T, Error> { ... }
    pub fn bytes_stream(self) -> impl Stream<Item = Result<Bytes, Error>> { ... }
}
```

## Key Considerations
1. **TLS**: Use rustls with native root certificates (no openssl)
2. **Timeout**: Support per-request timeout via asupersync Budget
3. **Streaming**: Response body must support async streaming for SSE
4. **Headers**: Support standard headers (Content-Type, Authorization, etc.)
5. **Error handling**: Wrap asupersync errors in pi's Error type

## Testing
- Unit test: Client creation succeeds
- Unit test: RequestBuilder builds correct request
- Integration test: GET request to httpbin.org/get
- Integration test: POST with JSON body
- Integration test: Timeout behavior

## Files
- src/http/client.rs (modify)
- src/http/mod.rs (update re-exports)
- src/error.rs (add HTTP error variants)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Client::new() creates working HTTP client
- [ ] GET/POST requests work
- [ ] JSON body serialization works
- [ ] Response streaming works
- [ ] Timeout respected
- [ ] TLS verification works

 {"id":"bd-ah1","title":"Implement session persistence cycle tests","description":"# Implement session persistence cycle tests

## Goal
Test the full session lifecycle: create → save → reload → verify integrity, with detailed logs.

## Background
Session persistence involves:
- JSONL file format (one JSON object per line)
- Header with metadata (version, cwd, timestamp)
- Message entries with parent/child relationships
- Tree structure for conversation branching
- File locking for concurrent access

Current tests cover serialization but not full I/O cycles.

## Runtime + Determinism
- Do not introduce new tokio-only tests; use `#[asupersync::test]` or a runtime wrapper.
- Use temp dirs and deterministic paths; avoid asserting exact timestamps.

## Logging Requirements
- Use TestLogger (bd-3ml).
- Log session path, header fields, message IDs, tree shape, and file sizes.
- On failure, dump the JSONL contents (redacted) and parsed header.

## Test Categories

### 1. Basic Persistence Cycle Tests
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_session_save_and_reload() {
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
    let session_path = temp.path().join("test.jsonl");

    let mut session = Session::new("/project/path");
    session.add_user_message("Hello");
    session.add_assistant_message("Hi there!");

    session.persist(&session_path).await.unwrap();

    let loaded = Session::load(&session_path).await.unwrap();
    assert_eq!(loaded.messages().len(), 2);
}
```

### 2. Tree Structure Persistence Tests
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_branching_persists() {
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
```

-{"id":"bd-btq","title":"Extensions: Node/Bun-free runtime (PiJS) — prove superior path","description":"# Goal
Deliver a Node/Bun-free extension runtime that is **more secure, more deterministic, and faster** than legacy Pi’s Node/Bun-based model, while still running representative JS extensions **as-is** via a thin compatibility layer.

# Background / Motivation
- Mario noted that **QuickJS lacks the OS API wrapper and event loop** provided by Node/Bun, which legacy extensions rely on.
- We reject bundling a full Node/Bun runtime; instead we supply a **minimal, capability-gated PiJS runtime** with a deterministic event loop and focused OS connectors.

# Strategy (PiJS)
1) **Connector-first architecture** — all I/O is explicit `host.call` into the connector dispatcher; no ambient OS APIs.
2) **Minimal JS stdlib** — a small, auditable PiJS standard library (`pi.fs`, `pi.http`, `pi.timers`, etc.) that maps to connectors.
3) **Deterministic event loop** — microtasks, timers, and hostcall promises driven by asupersync with strict ordering rules.
4) **Compatibility layer** — extc pipeline rewrites Node/Bun imports to PiJS shims + rejects forbidden APIs with actionable diagnostics.
5) **Security & performance proof** — capability gating + resource limits + benchmarks that show parity or improvement.

# Success Criteria
- A representative JS extension runs without Node/Bun.
- Deterministic ordering across repeated runs.
- Capability policy enforced for every hostcall with structured logs.
- Benchmarks show runtime overhead within budgets (or better).
- Documentation + evidence binder make the approach self-contained and reproducible.

# Non-Goals
- Implementing a full Node/Bun compatibility layer.
- Allowing unrestricted FS/network access.

# Notes for Future Self
This issue is the umbrella for the **"prove them wrong"** path. All implementation tasks should preserve: explicit capabilities, deterministic scheduling, auditable logs, and minimal surface area.  {"id":"bd-pwz","title":"Implement SSE streaming adapter for asupersync","description":"# Implement SSE streaming adapter for asupersync

## Goal
Bridge asupersync HTTP streaming response to the existing SSE parser (src/sse.rs).

## Background
The SSE parser in src/sse.rs is already complete and well-tested:
- SseParser::new() creates parser
- SseParser::feed(&str) returns Vec<SseEvent>
- Handles: event types, data fields, IDs, comments, multiline data
- 9 unit tests covering all edge cases

The challenge is connecting asupersync's streaming response body to this parser.

## Implementation

### Streaming Adapter
```rust
// src/http/sse.rs (new file or extend existing)

use crate::sse::{SseParser, SseEvent};
use asupersync::Cx;

pub struct SseStream {
    parser: SseParser,
    body: BodyReader,
    buffer: String,
}

impl SseStream {
    pub fn new(response: Response) -> Self {
        Self {
            parser: SseParser::new(),
            body: response.into_body(),
            buffer: String::new(),
        }
    }
    
    pub async fn next(&mut self, cx: &Cx) -> Option<Result<SseEvent, Error>> {
        loop {
            // Try to parse buffered data first
            let events = self.parser.feed(&self.buffer);
            if !events.is_empty() {
                self.buffer.clear();
                return Some(Ok(events.remove(0)));
            }
            
            // Read more data from body stream
            match self.body.read_chunk(cx).await {
                Ok(Some(chunk)) => {
                    self.buffer.push_str(&String::from_utf8_lossy(&chunk));
                }
                Ok(None) => return None,  // Stream ended
                Err(e) => return Some(Err(e.into())),
            }
        }
    }
}

// Or implement as a proper Stream trait for use with async iteration
impl Stream for SseStream {
    type Item = Result<SseEvent, Error>;
    
    fn poll_next(self: Pin<&mut Self>, cx: &mut Context<'_>) -> Poll<Option<Self::Item>> {
        // ...
    }
}
```

### Usage Pattern (in providers)
```rust
// In anthropic.rs
let response = client.post(url).json(&request).send(cx).await?;
let mut sse = SseStream::new(response);

while let Some(event) = sse.next(cx).await {
    match event? {
        SseEvent { data, event_type, .. } => {
            // Process Anthropic streaming event
        }
    }
}
```

## Considerations
1. **Backpressure**: Don't buffer entire response; process incrementally
2. **Error recovery**: Handle malformed events gracefully (log and continue)
3. **Cancellation**: Support clean shutdown via Cx cancellation
4. **Memory**: Limit buffer size to prevent OOM on malicious servers

## Dependencies
- bd-9sa (HTTP client wrapper must exist first)

## Testing
- Unit test: SseStream parses single event
- Unit test: SseStream handles multiline data
- Unit test: SseStream handles stream interruption
- Integration test: Stream from mock HTTP server

## Files
- src/http/sse.rs (new or extend)
- src/http/mod.rs (add re-export)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] SseStream wraps Response and yields SseEvent
- [ ] Incremental parsing works (no full buffering)

## Goal
Wire theme colors into lipgloss styles and glamour markdown rendering.

## Implementation

### Create Themed Styles
```rust
// src/interactive.rs

fn create_styles(theme: &Theme) -> Styles {
    Styles {
        message_user: lipgloss::Style::new()
            .foreground(lipgloss::Color::hex(&theme.colors.accent)),
        
        message_assistant: lipgloss::Style::new()
            .foreground(lipgloss::Color::hex(&theme.colors.foreground)),
        
        thinking: lipgloss::Style::new()
            .foreground(lipgloss::Color::hex(&theme.colors.muted))
            .italic(true),
        
        error: lipgloss::Style::new()
            .foreground(lipgloss::Color::hex(&theme.colors.error))
            .bold(true),
        
        success: lipgloss::Style::new()
            .foreground(lipgloss::Color::hex(&theme.colors.success)),
        
        border: lipgloss::Style::new()
            .border_foreground(lipgloss::Color::hex(&theme.ui.border)),
        
        // ... more styles
    }
}
```

### Apply to PiApp
```rust
impl PiApp {
    pub fn new(config: Config) -> Self {
        let theme = config.theme()
            .map(|name| Theme::load_by_name(&name).ok())
            .flatten()
            .unwrap_or_else(Theme::dark);
        
        let styles = create_styles(&theme);
        
        Self {
            theme,
            styles,
            // ...
        }
    }
}
```

### Apply to Glamour (Markdown)
```rust
fn create_glamour_style(theme: &Theme) -> glamour::Style {
    glamour::Style {
        document: glamour::StyleBlock {
            color: Some(theme.colors.foreground.clone()),
            background_color: Some(theme.colors.background.clone()),
            ..Default::default()
        },
        heading: glamour::StyleBlock {
            color: Some(theme.colors.accent.clone()),
            bold: Some(true),
            ..Default::default()
        },
        code: glamour::StyleBlock {
            color: Some(theme.syntax.string.clone()),
            ..Default::default()
        },
        // ... more elements
    }
}
```
